serving.description: 1 medium serving (approx. 100 g raw snake gourd)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw snake gourd is approximately 100 g, equivalent to roughly one cup of sliced pieces, as consumed in salads, stir-fries, or as a raw snack.

nutrient_source.title: Snake Gourd, Raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Foodo for snake gourd raw (cr_id F14040058). Secondary nutrients (sodium, sugar, saturated fat, potassium, vitamin C) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11647 (Snake gourd, raw) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Vegetables — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-GI Non-Starchy Vegetables (University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for snake gourd (Trichosanthes cucumerina) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Snake gourd is a non-starchy cucurbit vegetable with 2.5 g carbohydrate and 2.1 g fibre per 100 g. GI is estimated at approximately 40 (low) by analogy with comparable low-GI non-starchy vegetables (e.g., cucumber GI ~15, zucchini GI ~15, bitter gourd GI ~18–25) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (40 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.0 (very low). Traditional ethnobotanical literature also supports hypoglycaemic properties of snake gourd.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~3 mg/100 g) and high potassium (~391 mg/100 g) yield an excellent sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Snake gourd is an ideal DASH-diet vegetable for hypertensive individuals; consume regularly in its natural form and avoid adding high-sodium sauces or seasonings during preparation to preserve its favourable sodium-to-potassium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.5 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~40) support glycaemic control; traditional evidence for hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Snake gourd is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, high fibre, and low estimated glycaemic index; pair with lean protein sources to further moderate postprandial glucose response and avoid sugar-based or coconut-milk-heavy cooking preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium (391 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g) collectively support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Snake gourd is a heart-healthy vegetable with negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, meaningful potassium, and dietary fibre; it is appropriate for regular inclusion in a cardioprotective diet and should be prepared with minimal oil and no high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Snake gourd provides dietary fibre, vitamin C, and potassium with antioxidant properties but lacks sufficient omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or other established anti-inflammatory factors to be classified as actively beneficial for RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Snake gourd is not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis and its antioxidant micronutrients are supportive, but it does not provide sufficient anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols, >3 g whole-food fibre) to be classified as actively beneficial; pair with anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric or ginger and avoid high-saturated-fat or high-sodium cooking methods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Snake gourd (Trichosanthes cucumerina), known in Hindi as chichinda and in Tamil as pudalangai, is a long, slender, twisted cucurbit vegetable widely cultivated across South Asia and Southeast Asia, including India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, and Singapore. In its raw state it has a firm, crisp texture and a mild, slightly sweet flavour with a faint bitterness. The vegetable is predominantly water (approximately 94.6 g/100 g) and is extremely low in calories at only 18 kcal per 100 g, making it one of the lowest-calorie vegetables available. It provides 0.86 g protein, 0.07 g fat, and 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with 2.1 g dietary fibre contributing to satiety and digestive health. Sodium is negligible at approximately 3 mg/100 g while potassium is notably high at approximately 391 mg/100 g, making it an excellent DASH-diet vegetable for blood pressure management. Snake gourd is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, high fibre, and estimated low glycaemic index of approximately 40. It is also appropriate for cardiovascular health given its negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content. Traditionally, snake gourd has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for its purported hypoglycaemic, antipyretic, and laxative properties. It is commonly used in Indian curries, stir-fries, soups, and chutneys, and is also consumed raw in salads. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is snake gourd good for diabetes?
What are the nutrients in snake gourd?
Is snake gourd good for blood pressure?
How many calories in snake gourd?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore (cr_id F14040058, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central for snake gourd raw, which is an exact food match. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparators (non-starchy cucurbit vegetables) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore (F14040058) at confidence 0.95. 2. Secondary nutrients (sodium, sugar, saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, minerals, water) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11647 (Snake gourd, raw) as HPB does not report these fields. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible for raw vegetables per USDA). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero for plant foods). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero for plant foods). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero for this vegetable). 4. GI of approximately 40 is an analogous estimate based on comparable non-starchy cucurbit vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Trichosanthes cucumerina is available in the database as of 2026. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible analogous anchor for raw cucurbit vegetables; no exact FoodEx2 code for snake gourd exists as of 2024. 6. The rheumatoid_arthritis suitability from the input record was reclassified to 'moderate concern' in health_context additional_conditions, reflecting that snake gourd is not contraindicated but lacks sufficient established anti-inflammatory factors for a positive RA recommendation. 7. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as snake gourd is a South Asian and Southeast Asian vegetable commonly consumed in Singapore. Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese local names are not applicable as primary cuisine identifiers for this record.
